Adventure 8. To sleep. Per chance, to come up with great ideas. Hello, I'm Nancy. I guess you're looking for Tom, said a woman as I entered the garage. I assumed it was Tom's wife. He's just getting up from a nap and should be coming down in a minute. Thanks, I said. He's teaching me about the boxes. Nancy gave a knowing smile. Wonderful things, those boxes. Oh, do you use them? I asked. I certainly do. We could see my inquiring look, so quickly added. They've been all kinds of help to me around the house. Oh, I prodded further. She continued. Tom always seemed to have a knack for coming up with all kinds of ideas. I thought he was just born that way. But one day, the girls and I were in the kitchen making candies. We wanted to make chocolates with a sweet liquid filling, but we were having trouble getting the filling inside the hollow pieces of chocolate. We made all sorts of different holes, but we couldn't get the filling to go in unless we really heated the filling up. But then it melted the chocolate. Tom came by, looked over the problem and said, don't heat the filling. Freeze it. So we froze the filling into balls, dipped them in chocolate and voila! The chocolate hardened, the filling eventually melted back into liquid and we had our filled chocolates. Huh, I responded. I'd never have thought of that. I know. It never would have occurred to us in a million years to freeze the dang filling. I asked Tom how on earth he came up with it. When he told me he'd just done the opposite, I asked him to tell me more. Her voice, lower to a whisper. That was when he told me about the boxes and how he went through a few of them until he came to do the opposite, which, when he applied it, seemed to be the right solution. That was when I made him tell me what all the boxes were, and to this day, I still use those boxes to come up with ideas and solve all sorts of problems. I nodded, happy that we could talk about our shared admiration for her husband's brilliant idea boxes. Like a little while ago, our daughter wanted to start dating. She was definitely ready, but I certainly wasn't. Fortunately, I found something in virtually every one of the boxes that helped me deal with the idea of using my brain instead of my heart. How? I asked with great interest. Perhaps I thought to myself, these boxes could help improve my relationship with my own daughters, who were having a tough time of it since my wife had left us. Well, for example, divided up made me realize it wasn't just a question of dating or not dating. We could divide it up into stages. She could go on dates during the day before going on them at night. She could go on a date with other couples before she went on a date alone, and so on. In Do It Before Hand, I found many things we could do before the dating phase starts that made me more comfortable with the whole thing. We talked to her about boys, and girls, and dating. We agreed to limits. Both of us read about teen dating and discussed it afterward. And as a family, we watched a good romantic movie and talked about it afterward. And of course, change the interactions intensity had particular relevance. For example, rather than adding fuel to the fire by trying to exert parental authority, I realized I could, as Tom might say, create a more inert interaction by making sure she knows why she ought to be careful about situations where youthful passions might get out of control. That sounds much better than my plan, I admitted with a sly smile, which was to lock my daughters in the basement between their 14th and 21st birthdays. Nancy and I laughed. At that moment, Tom came through the door from the house to the garage with slightly puffy eyes. Who's locking who in the basement? He asked. I was just talking to your friend here about how I've been using your boxes, explained his wife. She gave him a sweet smile as she squeezed past him. Going out the same door, he had just come through. She called out behind her, nice talking with you. Good luck with your books and boxes. Her sing-songy voice echoed down the staircase. Tom looked as if he were still having trouble adjusting to being awake. Well, I teased him, all that inventing, where you out? Tom eyed me suspiciously. How much sleep do you get? He asked. Not enough, I replied. Six and a half hours a night or less. And unfortunately, no nap. Then I can tell you how to improve your creativity and problem solving ability quite literally overnight. Oh yeah? Get more sleep. Take naps. If I don't get enough sleep, a problem I usually solve in 15 minutes can take me an hour. A report I should be able to write in an hour usually takes me all day. Plus, I get some of my best ideas in my sleep. I screwed up my face. Hey, don't just take my word for it. He went on. You know that riff from the Rolling Stone song Satisfaction. It came to Keith Richards in his sleep. He woke up and found he had dictated it into a little recorder he kept by his bedside. Dimitri Mendeleev came up with the idea for the periodic table of elements when he saw them all organized in a dream. An August Cacule realized the breakthrough ring structure of the benzene molecule in his sleep dreaming about snakes biting their own tails. Tom was wide awake now and there was no stopping him. Elias Howe too dreamed up the idea for the sewing machine. He dreamt of savages surrounding him with spears preparing to cook him, which literally shaped his invention. But it's not just coming up with stuff in your sleep. Studies show that you're a whole lot more creative after you wake up and as much as three times better able to solve problems, possibly because your mind processes old junk and clears up processing space while you sleep. Maybe it's because you just get more stupid the more tired you get. I guess it doesn't really matter why does it. So I continued on his wavelength. Sleeping on it is a good approach to a problem. Absolutely. Just be sure you tell your mind you want it to work on solving whatever specific problem you want solved. It sounds ridiculous to talk to yourself like that, but it really works and never trying to solve an important problem if you haven't had enough sleep. Go take a nap instead. Okay, but please don't tell this to my employees. They'll be setting up cots in the storeroom and claim to be hard at work. Tom and I shared a chuckle. But enough about sleep. I interrupted myself rubbing my hands together. I'm ready for today's boxes. But Tom shook his head and shrugged. Sorry, I'm all out of boxes. Instead today, I want to show you something even better. Your final and most important lesson. But first, think of a problem you'd like to solve. To tell you the truth, I replied, my biggest problem right now is that I'm just overwhelmed with all the ideas you've helped me come up with. Good. That's a great problem to have. Now, I'd like a little fresh air to help me wake up. Why don't we go for a walk? I nodded in agreement and we both walked towards the door. Keep your problem in your mind, Tom added as we stepped outside the garage door and into a glorious spring day. Look around. What do you see? I see a beautiful day, I said, looking around. It was indeed a beautiful day, with the sun shining across a bright blue sky. Clear sky, sunny? It's spring and things are blooming. And keeping in mind the problem you'd like to solve, Tom reminded me, this is where I'd like you to start making some associations. Hmm, I said as I looked around some more. Our neighbor, Pat, waved to us from her garden. We waved back. Things blooming. Plants. Planting. I could plant some seeds. Take a few small steps on implementing a lot of ideas and see which ones blossom and bear fruit, and which ones don't. Good. Now, what's going on down there? Tom asked, pointing towards my feet. I looked down and noticed some ants at work in the dirt alongside the driveway, bending down to get closer. I could see them carrying particles of dirt out of the tunnel and neatly depositing them on the growing mound that encircled the hole. I became fascinated by the scene. Remember the problem, Tom reminded me again. He was right, too, as I had nearly forgotten about it. Well, like ants, I could take all the ideas and apply them one at a time, I answered. Pretty soon, I'd have a whole pile of good things happening. Yep, that's another possibility, Tom agreed. And looking up, I stood up and shielded my eyes from the sun's bright rays as I looked upwards. The sun, the sky, a single cloud floating along all by itself. Do you know why clouds float? asked Tom, ever the scientist. I think it's because the water molecules are so spread out and that makes them lighter than the air. Exactly. Any implications for your problem? Floating, floating to the surface. Maybe I could just kick all these ideas around with my staff and see which ones float to the surface, which ones we all get excited about and that the team agrees have the most potential. Silence fell as we continued walking. Tom, clearly giving me time to brainstorm, was carefully watching the sidewalk. Suddenly, something occurred to me. Hey, wait a minute. I thought I was only supposed to think inside those boxes. Tom must have known this was coming. He didn't flinch before replying, the boxes are a very useful guide. They're a systematic way of looking at things in new ways, but they're only tools. It's also important to know that you can stimulate and inspire your mind on your own without the boxes. And one of the best inspirations is Mother Nature's own designs and processes. Got the idea from a fellow named Alexander Graham Bell. At this point, Tom stopped and reached into his pocket, pulling out a folded up piece of paper. I carry his words around with me to guide me whenever I feel a bit lost. May I? I nodded, eager to hear the quote. Tom started to read, leave the beaten track occasionally and dive into the woods. Every time you do so, you will be certain to find something that you have never seen before. Follow it up, explore all around it. And before you know it, you will have something worth thinking about to occupy your mind. All really big discoveries are the results of thought. Of course, Tom continued, it doesn't have to be nature. You can be inspired by the streets of a city, the scene outside your window or the construction of a piece of furniture in your office. All that matters is that it gets you thinking in a new direction. Well, I replied, shoving my hands in my pockets sheepishly. You've certainly got me thinking in many new directions. In fact, I don't think I'll ever be able to think or look at anything in quite the same way again. Is there anything I can do for you in return? Any books you'd like, free coffee and pastry for life? Tom chuckled and batted the air. No, no, don't be silly. But I do appreciate the offer. He replied with a slight smile. After all, it's the thought that counts.
